Apple is planning to move production of iPhones it sells in the US to India, it has been reported . The technology giant has a strategy to diversify its supply chain but the ongoing tariff and trade war between China and the US is said to be speeding up that process . Apple is hoping to use India as the source for the more than 60 million iPhones sold annually in US by the end of 2026 .  Experts have already warned the price of electronics generally, and not just Apple products, are likely to rise . The iPhone maker is set to report its latest financial results next week .

Back on April Fools' Day, there were rumors about Warren Buffett acquiringTesla for $1 trillion and taking it over . While Tesla is a leading company in the electric vehicle (EV) market, it's simply not the type of stock I would ever expect Buffett to buy .  Buffett's favorite stocks have strongcompetitive moat that enable them to dominate their respective industries . He refers to this as its moat . Others face significant competition and will have a hard time separating themselves from the pack .  Tech isn't Buffett's specialty and while there may be the oddtech companywithin Berkshire's portfolio, those types of purchases likely haven't been made by Buffett himself, but rather others on his team . Buffett advocates for staying within your circle of competence and not wading out too far into uncharted waters, where risk is often greater .  If you're a tech investor or are bullish on EVs, then odds are you probably wouldn't see Buffett owning stocks that you might buy . Ultimately, it's important to focus on your own needs and strengths as an investor; they won't be the same as Buffett's .  On rare occasions, our expert team of analysts issues a“Double Down” stockrecommendation for companies that they think are about to pop . Nvidia:if you invested $1,000 when we doubled down in 2009,you’d have $266,353!* Apple: If you’ve already missed your chance to invest, now is the best time to buy before it’s too late .  Warren Buffett isn't likely to buy Tesla stock -- ever -- ever . Warren Buffett is against Tesla stock, but he's not sure he'll buy it ever .

Berkshire Hathaway CEO Warren Buffett has demonstrated a knack for handily outperforming Wall Street's benchmark index, theS&P 500 . Buffett doesn't offer stock-specific recommendations, but his trades do the talking . Five brand-name stocks account for nearly 64% of Berkshire's $268 billion of invested assets .  Berkshire Hathaway's five largest holdings, as a percentage of invested assets, were Apple . Coca-Cola and American Express are two of what Buffett considers to be "indefinite" holdings .  The bulk of Berkshire's Bank of America stake was built up by exercising warrants for 700 million shares at a price of $7.14 per share in the summer of 2017 . American Express has been a steady holding since 1991, while Coca-Cola has been held continuously since 1988 .  The Oracle of Omaha has put more than $47 billion of his company's cash to work in brand-name businesses . But this is still $30 billion less than he's invested in his favorite stock since the midpoint of 2018 . The interesting quirk about what I'm calling Warren Buffett's favorite stock to buy is that you won't find it listed in Berkshire's quarterly 13F filings .  On July 17, 2018, Berkshire Hathaway's board amended the share repurchase rules to give its CEO more liberty to reward investors via buybacks . The new criteria allow Buffett to repurchase as many shares as he likes, with no ceiling or end date, as long as Berkshire has at least $30 billion in combined cash, cash equivalents, and U.S. Treasuries on its balance sheet .  Nearly $78 billion worth of buybacks in less than seven years signals Buffett's utmost confidence in the company he built with Munger over many decades . Recently, Berkshire Hathaway stock has been trading at its highest price-to-book multiple since 2008 .  Berkshire Hathaway wasn’t one of the 10 best stocks to buy now . Apple is jettisoning the product's current issuer, a change that might result in a switch of the network operator facilitating the card's transactions . The stakes in this battle could be high . A great many Apple Card holders are also Apple device owners .  The issuer behind the card was, and remains for now, Goldman Sachs . At the time Goldman was pushing hard into consumer finance, most notably starting a next-generation bank called Marcus . The company began transitioning out of Marcus in 2022 .  Apple Card's network operator since inception has been Mastercard . Mastercard's archrival Visa has offered around $100 million to Apple to be the facilitator of Apple Card transactions . American Express also seems to be in the running for Apple's business .  An estimate from a venture capital firm based on those 2024 numbers calculated annual Apple Card spend of $67 billion, equating to roughly $5,500 per user . Delta Air LinesSkyMiles cards underpinned by AmEx produced $250 billion worth of spend on a narrower base of 7.5 million cards .  Apple might have to relax its requirements if it wants a robust and mutually beneficial partnership . Visa is a powerhouse in the networking segment and has much leverage on that basis alone . JPMorgan Chase is the largest U.S. credit card issuer by payment volume -- of over $1.3 trillion .  Apple is clearly determined to push deeper into consumer finance . Given that, plus the fact that the Goldman arrangement eventually tanked, it might well be amenable to offering conciliatory terms to potential new partners . This will be the tell of which company or companies will ultimately walk away with the biggest prizes . China’s government is considering suspending its 125% tariff on some US imports . Authorities are considering removing the additional levies for medical equipment and some industrial chemicals like ethane . Officials are also discussing waiving the tariff for plane leases .  The possibility of some goods being exempted boosted investor optimism, as shares in Asia rose and the yuan erased losses . The exemptions mirror similar moves on the part of the US, which excluded electronics from its 145% tariff on Chinese imports earlier this month .  China is the world’s largest plastics manufacturer but some of its factories depend on ethane, which is mainly imported from the US . The list of exemptions is still in flux and discussions may not progress .  Traders have been circulating purportedly tariff-exempt lists of customs codes that correlate to key chemicals and chip-making components . Beijing is also preparing to waive additional tariffs on at least eight semiconductor-related products, Caijing reported .  Investors are looking for signs that the two countries will engage to lower tariffs . On Thursday, Chinese officials publicly demanded the US revoke all unilateral tariffs before agreeing to trade talks . On the US front, Trump’s administration has exempted smartphones, computers and other electronics from its so-called reciprocal tariffs .  The exclusions apply to smartphones, laptop computers, hard drives and computer processors and memory chips as well as flat-screen displays . Manufacturers including Apple Inc. and Nvidia Corp. include Apple and Nvidia .

Apple aims to import most of its iPhones from India by the end of next year . The goal means Apple will need to roughly double its iPhone output in India . Apple sells more than 60 million iPhones a year in the US .  Apple assembled $22 billion worth of iPhones in India in the 12 months ended March, increasing production by nearly 60% over the previous year . The bulk of India-made iPhones are assembled at Foxconn Technology Group’s factory in southern India . Apple representatives in India didn’t immediately respond to a request for comment .  Apple exported 1.5 trillion rupees ($17.5 billion) in iPhones from the region in the fiscal year through March 2025 . Shipments of iPhones from India to the US accelerated after Trump announced his plans for the “reciprocal” tariffs in February .  Apple now assembles its entire iPhone range in India, including the more expensive titanium Pro models . State subsidies tied to Prime Minister Narendra Modi’s ambition to turn the country into a manufacturing hub .

Soriot: Europe needs to invest more in what really matters to it . Europe spends a substantially lower share of GDP on innovative medicines than the US, he said .  Despite nervousness around tariffs, AstraZeneca shares are down less than 1% year-to-date . Company posted a 21% increase in revenue for 2024 at $54.1bn and a 29% rise in reported earnings per share at $4.54 . Ricky Mulvey: It does really speak to what Google has been able to achieve over the last decades, just becoming the search engine of choice for most. of course. But I think some out there would certainly find ways to benefit .  Google is essentially forbidden from making the deals and investments required to keep winning, Moser says . Moser: "I'm not one to hold a company's success against it. I think I encourage that"  Apple could lose the easiest $20 billion that a company can make. That's what Google pays Apple to be the default search engine. But there's a lot of other big tech observers seeing what's happening here. What ripple effects are you watching?  Ricky Mulvey: I think these are the signs that Big Tech is really under the microscope . Ricky: It's going to be very interesting to follow how all of these companies fall into these anti-trust examinations because it's not like Google is doing anything that a lot of companies aren't . Jason Moser: If you have an impact like that, and it slows down the growth story even more for investors .  GM is investing heavily in its own battery technology and ultium . Ricky Mulvey: GM doesn't like the way incentives are structured for EV leases are structured . Ricky: There's stuff going on in this market, especially in Colorado that's just weird . Jason Moser: GM is trying to drive down costs and increase production efficiency .  Jason Moser: Even when I was getting that, I was like, There's no way this thing is going to last forever. This really doesn't feel sustainable, but there's a lot of work going on behind the scenes . Ricky Mulvey: You're doing what you can to get it out there, create demand, and then hopefully down the road, consumers see the virtue, the value .  There are a lot of upfront costs that come with developing these vehicles and ultimately getting them out to market . Battery costs, of course, have always been a real issue, and those costs are coming down . They want to be seen as a credible EV provider, and that just takes time to do .  There's a bank shot here, which is that as Elon Musk becomes more politically divisive, fewer people tend to be buying Teslas . At the same time, last year, General Motors went from 6% of the EV market share in the United States at the beginning of 2024 to 12% by the end .  Musk's actions have had an impact on the business over the last year or so. significantly, and that's compared to, I think, around 24% with an actual positive or 27% . I think that really shows the dangers of business leaders wearing their politics on their sleeves. You just got to understand that does come with a cost.  As an automaker, you want to be able to open yourself up to the widest market possible . It's not going to be all EVs and no gas. Maybe years from now, that'll be the case. But I think today we're looking at some folks want EVs, some want hybrids .  Bill Ackman's hedge fund is buying up about 20% of Hertz . Ricky Mulvey: It's just I'm not really an auto stock guy. I love my car. To me, autos aren't the most exciting market opportunity .  Ackman sees Hertz as a $30 stock . Ackman feels like there's a return to what he called rational consumer behavior . Ricky Mulvey: Ackman likes posting on X.com and gives out his thesis .  Ricky Mulvey: Any advice to retail investors that want to tail this investor on a turnaround story? Jason Moser: "It's always worth your while to do your own work and come to your own decisions" Ricky: "This isn't a company that he plans to hold onto for a long time. It seems more of a value thesis, where he sees a short term catalyst maybe offering an attractive risk reward situation"  Robert Brokamp answers some of the questions you emailed us about tariffs, 401(k)s, and backdoor Roths . Ricky Mulvey: The first question comes from Fool Up North .  US Customs and Border Protection, otherwise known as CBP, classifies the item and assesses any tariffs . CBP employs more than 60,000 people to monitor the 320 official ports of entry into the US .  The tariff is paid by the company that imports the goods, which most often is a US company . The foreign company that made the item does not pay the tariff . The money goes to the US Treasury and goes into what's called the general fund .  Anonymous, you are not a lesser Fool, but we will still answer your question . Anonymous: Can you explain more about what a backdoor Roth IRA contribution is? Robert Brokamp: That is very true .  If you make too much to contribute directly to a Roth IRA, you might consider doing what's known as the backdoor Roth . You contribute to a nondeductible, traditional IRA, and then very soon after you convert it to Roth . There are really little to no tax consequences if that's your only traditional IRA .  Money in a 401(k) is not considered when it comes to the pro rata rules for IRA conversions . If you do a Roth conversion, even if it's in just one of your many IRAs, 10% will be tax free but 90% is taxable .  Can a parent open Roth IRAs for grandparents with a grandson as the beneficiary, then use that money to pay for college? Robert Brokamp: No. They could do it if they have earned income, and that is income from a job, so not interest, dividends, capital gain, Social Security, pension, anything like that .  401(k), Roth IRA can be withdrawn, tax and penalty free at any time . IRAs can be used for qualified higher education expenses for the IRA owner, their spouse, children or grandchildren . A likely better way to do all of this would be for the grandparents to contribute to a 529 college savings plan .  Most financial planners recommend the 529 over and Roth IRA when it comes to saving for collage . The total that is gradually rolled over can't exceed $35,000, and the account has to have been open for at least 15 years .  Is there a book on the origin story of the 401(k? I'm curious why pensions disappeared and how we got here with self funding retirement? Robert Brokamp: There are a few books, Karen, but I'll instead recommend a podcast episode of Motley Fool Answers .  The first 401(k was created on January 1, 1981 . It took a few years for it to catch on, but then when it did, it just took off . It's just easier for a company to move from traditional pensions to defined contribution plans .  Karen says most people, once they get to a certain age, wish they had saved more . Karen says 401(k) system is too complex for people to decide how much to save, how to invest and how to retire . Karen: It's just easier they decide, that's just too complex .  A potential new employer offers an 8% contribution of your pay after the first year . Ricky Mulvey: You should be saving probably 15% of your household income for retirement that includes the match . The days of traditional pensions just aren't coming back .  Robert Brokamp: These plans aren't very common, but are similar to a 401(k), except that the employer makes the contribution . Each company will have its own criteria, but in some cases, if the firm reaches a certain level of profitability, all eligible employees get money deposited in their accounts .  NFL players have a capital accumulation plan . Their team deposits the money, and it invests after three seasons . Withdrawals will be taxed as ordinary income and withdrawals before age 59.5 .  Ricky Mulvey: Any advice on how to stay in the NFL for more than three seasons? Robert Brokamp:I'll call up my friend Tom Brady later on, and I'll pass along what he says .  Jason Moser and Ricky Mulvey are directors of directors of Charles Schwab and The Trade Desk . The Motley Fool has positions in Alphabet, Apple, Meta Platforms, Tesla, and Uber Technologies .

Apple plans to relocate the robotics team from John Giannandrea’s AI organization to the hardware division later this month . That will place it under Senior Vice President John Ternus, who oversees hardware engineering . The pending shift will mark the second major project to be removed in the past month .  The Siri engineering unit was taken over by Mike Rockwell, who previously ran hardware and software development for the Vision Pro headset . He is replacing much of the management of Siri with top deputies from the visionOS team, Bloomberg News reported this week .  The group is led by veteran executive Kevin Lynch, who has managed Apple Watch software and the now-defunct self-driving car initiative . Apple plans to release a tabletop robot that uses an artificial limb to move around an iPad-like display . For further in the future, the group has discussed building mobile machines, including a roaming robot similar to the Amazon Astro .  Ternus is in charge of hardware engineering for the iPhone, iPad, Mac, Vision Pro and most other products . He's one of Tim Cook’s most trusted lieutenants . Many employees believe he could be the next CEO .  Giannandrea has lost hundreds of engineers just this year . Apple has been reeling after major delays to key Siri features and a tepid response to the Apple Intelligence platform .  Apple’s former head of Siri under Giannandrea called the situation “ugly” and “embarrassing” at an all-hands meeting last month . The company may be preparing for a world without the executive at the helm of its AI efforts . A breakup of the AI and ML team is looking more likely, people said .

Consumer Reports evaluated a half dozen digital wallets over a 10-month period ending in March . The wallets were from Apple, Cash App, Google, PayPal Holdings, Samsung and Venmo . All six allow consumers to make contactless payments and peer-to-peer money transfers .  Apple scored highest for the safety of its stored balance and P2P functions . Four of the wallets – from Apple, Cash App, Google and PayPal – were rated broadly equal overall for their wallet and pay functions .  PayPal and Venmo offer “slightly more generous liability coverage for unauthorized transactions” than Block’s Cash App, Consumer Reports says . The disclosure on their legal obligations “was relatively clear and transparent,” the report found . “All the companies, fintech or traditional tech, have more economic incentive to proactively address and provide stronger consumer protections, the report says .  Roughly 59% of people use a digital wallet at least once per month, survey finds . Apple Wallet was the most often used (39%) followed by PayPal (16%) and Google (14%) Congress has repealed a rule that would have given the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau regulatory oversight of digital wallet providers .  All of the wallets offer overlapping functions, but Google is the only one of the six not to offer peer-to-peer payments . Google is also the only company among its wallet peers not to require binding arbitration to resolve legal disputes .  Consumer Reports’ overview was supported financially by Craig Newmark Philanthropies and Flourish Ventures, a San Francisco-based investment firm .
